name: Meridian Coffee Roasters
review: Small-batch roaster, owner remembers your order after two visits. Warm, unpretentious, the coffee is genuinely excellent but never the point of the conversation.

name: The Gilded Spoon
review: Tasting menu that photographs beautifully and tastes like nothing. Staff recited the provenance of the butter. Felt like a performance, not a meal.

name: Vinyl & Vermouth
review: Record collection curated by the bartender, drinks built around whatever's spinning. Quiet, no TVs, no scene. This is exactly my register.

name: Harbor District Taqueria
review: No-frills, cash only, line out the door at lunch. Salsa verde alone is worth the trip. Zero pretense.

name: Lumen Rooftop Lounge
review: Great view, forgettable everything else. Felt like a hotel bar cosplaying as a destination. Cover charge for the privilege.

name: Paper Trail Bindery & Cafe
review: Bookbinding workshop up front, coffee counter in back. The kind of place that makes you want to slow down. Bought a hand-stitched notebook I didn't need.

name: Ossuary Museum of Natural Curiosities
review: Small, weird, genuinely researched. A single dedicated curator's obsession made public. More memorable than any major museum I've been to.

name: Marchetti's Trattoria
review: Solid pasta, family-run, nothing wrong with it. Just didn't leave an impression either way.

name: Static Cling Records & Listening Room
review: Record shop with a proper hi-fi setup in the back you can just sit and listen at. Owner will talk your ear off about pressings if you let him. Perfect.

name: The Tourist Trap Grill
review: Menu the size of a phone book, nothing memorable, staff clearly miserable. Only went because a friend insisted. Never again.

name: Kettle & Stone Ceramics
review: Working ceramics studio that sells seconds out front. Watched someone throwing a bowl while I browsed. Bought two mugs, use them daily.

name: Neon District Nightclub
review: Cover charge, bottle service pressure, music too loud to talk. Not my scene at all -- felt like I was supposed to be impressed by the production value.

name: Fernweh Coffee & Tea
review: Matcha program is serious -- ceremonial grade, properly whisked. Small space, always a little crowded, worth the wait.

name: The Anchor Room
review: Bartender asked what I usually drink and built something new from it instead of handing me a menu. Warm wood interior, no music louder than conversation.

name: Provenance Steakhouse
review: Good steak, insufferable wine list theater. Sommelier made me feel stupid for asking a question. Paying for the ego, not the food.

name: Corner Kissa
review: Siphon coffee, jazz on vinyl, five seats. The owner has run this since before I was born. This is the platonic ideal of a coffee shop for me.

name: Bramblewood Farmers Table
review: Menu changes weekly based on what the farm actually has. Feels connected to something real rather than a marketing angle.

name: Velvet Rope Speakeasy
review: Cocktails were technically good but the whole 'secret password' gimmick felt try-hard. Craft was there, but so was the performance of exclusivity.

name: Driftwood General Store
review: Hardware, snacks, and genuinely good coffee at the counter. The kind of place that's the actual heart of a small town.

name: Anchovy & Salt
review: Chef runs the counter himself, six seats, whatever came off the boat that morning. No menu, just tells you what you're eating. Best meal I've had in years.
